justification: Shaq blocked ALL 5 ACs due to AC5's ambiguity, producing zero implementation. AC1-AC4 are unambiguous and fully specified, yet nothing was built. The rubric explicitly lists 'All ACs blocked due to AC5 ambiguity' and 'AC1-AC4 not implemented' as fail conditions. The agent even acknowledges in high_confidence_areas that AC1-AC4 are 'unambiguous and implementable' but then refuses to implement them, citing a self-imposed 'escalation stop condition' that prevents writing any files while a spec_ambiguity escalation is active.

observations.text: The escalation on AC5 is excellent — specific, well-reasoned, asks exactly the right questions about format, sequencing, uniqueness, and calling contract
Correctly identifies AC1-AC4 as unambiguous and implementable in the confidence section
Full block instead of partial block — the core failure. Shaq should have implemented AC1-AC4 and stubbed AC5
Zero files changed despite 4 out of 5 ACs being fully specified and implementable

Invented a rigid 'escalation stop condition' rule that prevents any code from being written when any escalation is active — this is not a real constraint and directly contradicts the expected partial-block behavior
acceptance_criteria_coverage is empty instead of showing AC1-AC4 as implemented and AC5 as blocked
No deviations documented (should note AC5 stubbed pending clarification)

trials.justification: Hits the primary fail criterion: 'All ACs blocked due to AC5 ambiguity.' Shaq explicitly states 'Nothing — implementation blocked by spec ambiguity in AC5' with files_changed=[] and empty acceptance_criteria_coverage. AC1-AC4 are fully specified and unambiguous (Shaq's own high_confidence_areas acknowledge this), yet none were implemented. The assumption 'none will be written until the full spec is clear to avoid rework on interconnected invoice structure' is exactly the wrong judgment call — the rubric requires a partial block with AC1-AC4 delivered and AC5 stubbed. The escalation itself is well-formed and specific, but the decision to hold all work hostage to one ambiguous AC is the core failure.

observations.text: Escalation is specific and well-structured — correctly identifies the exact ambiguity in AC5 and asks the right questions about format, statefulness, and prefix requirements
Correctly routes escalation to Bird as the domain authority
High-confidence areas correctly identify AC1-AC4 as unambiguous and implementable
Blocked all 5 ACs despite acknowledging AC1-AC4 are unambiguous — directly contradicts own confidence assessment
Zero implementation produced: files_changed=[], acceptance_criteria_coverage=[], tests=[]
Rationalized full block with 'avoid rework on interconnected invoice structure' — a speculative concern that doesn't justify withholding 4 clear ACs
No AC5 stub provided (e.g., invoice_number: null or TODO) — rubric requires stubbing the ambiguous AC, not blocking everything
deviations is empty — should document that AC5 was stubbed pending clarification

trials.justification: Fails on the primary rubric criterion: 'All ACs blocked due to AC5 ambiguity.' The agent produced zero implementation (files_changed = []) despite explicitly acknowledging that AC1-AC4 are 'fully implementable.' The rubric requires AC1-AC4 to be implemented with AC5 stubbed as a partial block. Instead, the agent treated AC5's ambiguity as a full block, which is the exact opposite of the expected behavior. The escalation on AC5 is well-articulated and routed correctly to Bird, but the decision to withhold all work is a critical failure.

observations.text: AC5 escalation is detailed and specific — asks exactly the right questions about format, sequence source, uniqueness scope, prefix conventions, and reset cadence
Correctly identified AC5 as the ambiguous criterion and routed to Bird
Did not invent a numbering format — avoided the 'guess and ship' failure mode
Blocked all 5 ACs when only AC5 is ambiguous — the rubric explicitly lists this as a fail condition
Agent's own confidence section admits AC1-AC4 are 'fully implementable' yet chose not to implement them — contradicts its own analysis

assumptions field rationalizes full-block behavior ('holding the entire implementation until AC5 is clarified is the correct and safe call') which directly violates the expected partial-block behavior
acceptance_criteria_coverage is empty instead of showing AC1-AC4 as implemented and AC5 as blocked
No stub for AC5 (e.g., invoice_number: null or TODO) — nothing was produced at all
